response_text:
:::writing
The public pool in my town sat behind the middle school, down a cracked asphalt drive that smelled faintly of tar in the summer heat. If you didn’t know it was there, you could drive past a hundred times and never notice it. A low chain-link fence, a fading sign about swim hours, a rectangle of blue hidden behind cottonwood trees. But for three months each year, it became the most important place in town.

You could tell a lot about people by when they showed up.

Mornings belonged to the lap swimmers. They arrived with canvas bags and quiet determination, slipping into the water just after the gates opened. They were mostly retirees and a few people who worked odd shifts at the hospital or the grain elevator. They didn’t talk much. The pool surface stayed smooth, cut by steady, practiced strokes. Occasionally someone would pause at the wall and mention the weather, or the price of corn, or a knee that wasn’t what it used to be.

By late morning the swim lessons began. This was when the nervous energy arrived—small kids clutching foam kickboards, parents sitting along the concrete edge pretending they weren’t watching every second. The lifeguards were teenagers who had grown up in the same lessons a decade earlier. They blew whistles with a seriousness that suggested they were guarding the English Channel instead of four feet of chlorinated water.

The pool taught kids a particular kind of bravery. The diving board, for instance, revealed exactly how old you were—not by your birthday, but by how long you stood at the edge deciding whether to jump. Every town had that one kid who ran to the end and flipped without hesitation. Everyone else climbed the ladder slowly and stared down at the water like it was an ocean trench.

What I remember most is the afternoon shift.

By two o’clock the place filled with the full social map of the town. Construction workers came straight from job sites, still smelling like sawdust and sun. Moms arrived with coolers and towels. A group of older boys from the baseball team claimed the deep end like it was territory.
